articles.title: After hospitals, patients get a turn to bring AI into the doctor's office
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Health tech After hospitals, patients get a turn to bring AI into the doctor’s office Developers pitch their patient apps as diligent note-takers to keep track of encounters across the health system Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Katie Palmer June 4, 2026 Health Tech Correspondent Katie Palmer katie.palmer@statnews.com Katie covers the impact of health technology on patients, clinicians, and businesses. Her stories explore the price tag of clinical AI, digital health at the FDA, and the boom in direct-to-consumer telehealth. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at palmer.01. Get email alerts for Katie Palmer Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Patients are getting used to being recorded in the doctor’s office. More than a quarter of U.S. practices now use AI-based listening tools called ambient scribes, which capture visits in real time and draft clinical notes for clinicians to enter into patients’ medical records. But what happens when it’s the patient doing the recording? Advertisement Nearly every patient now has a recording device in their pocket, and commercial large language models have made it easier than ever for them to transcribe and interpret audio files. A growing number of consumer apps — with names like VisitRecall, Advoca Health, and AlignCare — are using AI to do just that, delivering summaries and action items for patients after the fact. One called Kin Health, with backing from the co-founder of GoodRx, announced a $9 million seed round in May. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. Subscribe Log In Artificial intelligence , health tech , patients , STAT+ Submit a correction request Reprints Katie Palmer Health Tech Correspondent Katie covers the impact of health technology on patients, clinicians, and businesses. Her stories explore the price tag of clinical AI, digital health at the FDA, and the boom in direct-to-consumer telehealth. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at palmer.01. articles.title: Lawmakers promised cancer patients would be protected from Medicaid cuts. Now CMS says otherwise
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion Lawmakers promised cancer patients would be protected from Medicaid cuts. Now CMS says otherwise New work requirements are catastrophic Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Chris Hondros/Getty Images By Gwen Nichols June 4, 2026 Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United. In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions. One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition. Advertisement Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage. advocacy , Cancer , CMS , HHS , Medicaid Submit a correction request Reprints Gwen Nichols Newsletter The smartest thinkers in life sciences on what's happening — and what's to come Advertisement Most Popular Top ultra-processed food researchers call for sweeping policy change: ‘The system is rigged’ Revolution Medicines starts shipping experimental pancreatic cancer drug Trump administration to strip job protections of top NIH officials, grants staff Longevity startup NewLimit raises $435 million ahead of first clinical trial The virtual end of the doctor’s office waiting room 2025 Edition: Ranking Biotech’s Top Venture Capital Firms Advertisement STAT@ASCO: Science vs. Cancer Chicago, IL May 29 ASCO 2026: The STAT Recap Virtual Jun 3 2026 Breakthrough Summit West San Francisco, CA May 19 Most Read in STAT+ Revolution Medicines starts shipping experimental pancreatic cancer drug For prostate cancer patients set on surgery, new hormone regimen may improve outcomes, study finds Trump administration seeks to overhaul federal grantmaking process, alarming researchers Blood pressure tech floods the market after FDA relaxes wearables oversight Practice-changing results reported for Revolution Medicines pancreatic cancer drug Recommended First Opinion First Opinion June 4, 2026 Grail’s multi-cancer early detection trial was negative. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition. By Gwen Nichols June 4, 2026 Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United.
By Gwen Nichols
Gwen Nichols
June 4, 2026
Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United.
In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions. One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition. Advertisement Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage.

Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions.

One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition.

Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage.

articles.text:

STAT Plus Newsletter AI Prognosis What the pope’s encyclical on AI means for Catholic hospitals, and all of health care You’re reading the web edition of STAT AI Prognosis Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article STAT/ Alberto PIZZOLI / AFP via Getty Images By Brittany Trang June 3, 2026 Health Tech Reporter Brittany Trang brittany.trang@statnews.com Brittany Trang, Ph.D., covers AI in health and medicine: Does it actually work? Who benefits, or might be harmed? She writes the weekly AI Prognosis newsletter . Follow her on Threads , Mastodon , and Bluesky . You can reach Brittany on Signal at btrang.01. Get email alerts for Brittany Trang Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Y ou’re reading the web edition of STAT’s AI Prognosis newsletter, our subscriber-exclusive guide to artificial intelligence in health care and medicine. Sign up to get it delivered in your inbox every Wednesday. The sequel to last week’s graduation speeches that garnered boos for AI: Comedian Ronny Chieng addressed Harvard’s graduating class and told them their mission was to “destroy AI,” which prompted a roar of approval. Advertisement He wasn’t referring to uses of AI in medicine and physics, he specified, but rather offloading writing and creating to AI. The journey of making and learning and figuring out is “the point of all of this,” he said. “Why would I want AI to take that away from me?” To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: The medical-billing AI arms race between providers and insurance
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion The medical-billing AI arms race between providers and insurance Both sides are training algorithms to fight over the same patient record Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Darshak Sanghavi June 3, 2026 Sanghavi, a pediatric cardiologist and former senior federal health official, is chief medical officer of Machinify. In November 2020, Bisi Bennett went into labor at seven months pregnant. Halfway to AdventHealth hospital, with her husband, Chris, behind the wheel, she gave birth to their son Dorian in their Mitsubishi Outlander. He didn’t breathe. At the hospital, before they wheeled Bisi away from her newborn, she heard four magical words: “We’ve got a pulse.” Dorian spent 56 days in the neonatal intensive care unit getting respiratory support, surgery, cardiology, and nutritional care, and thrived. The doctors and nurses had, by any measure, performed a small miracle. Advertisement It’s hard to put a price on a miracle. But technology has increasingly taken on the challenge. Opinion First Opinion The medical-billing AI arms race between providers and insurance Both sides are training algorithms to fight over the same patient record Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Darshak Sanghavi June 3, 2026 Sanghavi, a pediatric cardiologist and former senior federal health official, is chief medical officer of Machinify. In November 2020, Bisi Bennett went into labor at seven months pregnant. Halfway to AdventHealth hospital, with her husband, Chris, behind the wheel, she gave birth to their son Dorian in their Mitsubishi Outlander. He didn’t breathe. At the hospital, before they wheeled Bisi away from her newborn, she heard four magical words: “We’ve got a pulse.” Dorian spent 56 days in the neonatal intensive care unit getting respiratory support, surgery, cardiology, and nutritional care, and thrived. The doctors and nurses had, by any measure, performed a small miracle. Advertisement It’s hard to put a price on a miracle. But technology has increasingly taken on the challenge. articles.title: Trump's Medicaid work requirements have an unwelcome surprise for some states and patients
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Politics Trump’s Medicaid work requirements have an unwelcome surprise for some states and patients New rules will make it far harder to get Medicaid and stay covered Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Damian Dovarganes/AP By Tara Bannow , Chelsea Cirruzzo ,  and Daniel Payne June 3, 2026 Tara Bannow tara.bannow@statnews.com Tara covers the business of health care. Her stories focus on hospitals, doctors, and how their business practices affect patients — especially when private equity gets involved. She also writes about health insurance and ideas for improving our broken health system. You can reach Tara on Signal at tarabannow.70. Get email alerts for Tara Bannow Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Chelsea Cirruzzo chelsea.cirruzzo@statnews.com Chelsea covers the Department of Health and Human Services with a particular focus on power and influence at the top. Confidential tips can be sent via Signal at chelseacirruzzo.42. Get email alerts for Chelsea Cirruzzo Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Daniel Payne daniel.payne@statnews.com Daniel covers the intersection of the health industry and the federal government. He reports on corporate influence in government, the health consequences of federal policies, and the politics of health care. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at danielp.100. Get email alerts for Daniel Payne Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Medicaid leaders and advocates say they’re shocked by the Trump administration’s harsh directives for implementing Medicaid work requirements, which they say mark a pivot from how federal officials had characterized their plans just weeks ago. Much of the conversation around the nearly 400-page rule that the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services released Monday afternoon centers on one of the ways that people can be exempted from work requirements: medical frailty. Getting that exemption will be more difficult than most people had expected, meaning that more sick and disabled people are likely to lose their Medicaid coverage. Advertisement “This is where we’ll see large and harmful coverage losses,” said Benjamin Sommers, an economics professor at the Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health. “This is a population that has high medical needs and is at major risk for harm if they lose coverage. That is the headline implication of the new rule.” To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. Medicaid leaders and advocates say they’re shocked by the Trump administration’s harsh directives for implementing Medicaid work requirements, which they say mark a pivot from how federal officials had characterized their plans just weeks ago. Much of the conversation around the nearly 400-page rule that the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services released Monday afternoon centers on one of the ways that people can be exempted from work requirements: medical frailty. Getting that exemption will be more difficult than most people had expected, meaning that more sick and disabled people are likely to lose their Medicaid coverage. Advertisement “This is where we’ll see large and harmful coverage losses,” said Benjamin Sommers, an economics professor at the Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health. “This is a population that has high medical needs and is at major risk for harm if they lose coverage. That is the headline implication of the new rule.” To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. Subscribe Log In

articles.title: Online care is caught in the crossfire as states crack down on corporate medicine
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Thumy Phan for STAT STAT Plus Health tech Online care is caught in the crossfire as states crack down on corporate medicine By Katie Palmer June 2, 2026 Palmer is investigating how telehealth is driving the consumerization of drugs. This is Part 5 of the series The VirtualRx Boom. Katie Palmer katie.palmer@statnews.com Katie covers the impact of health technology on patients, clinicians, and businesses. Her stories explore the price tag of clinical AI, digital health at the FDA, and the boom in direct-to-consumer telehealth. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at palmer.01. Get email alerts for Katie Palmer Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article I In less than a decade, telehealth has expanded from a sideshow of health care to an industry worth tens of billions of dollars. Companies like Hims & Hers and Teladoc have become household names, their ads interrupting streaming TV and flooding social media feeds with the promise of quick, convenient care. Despite their popularity, few patients understand who’s actually taking care of them when they click through a telehealth site. Advertisement National telehealth brands present a unified front to patients across the country. But in more than 30 states, it’s illegal for corporations to practice medicine. So behind the scenes, telehealth companies work with distinct, independent medical groups. Owned by physicians — who often hold 50 state licenses at once — those practices are meant to act as a firewall, making sure that clinical decisions are driven by patients’ needs, not the profits of the corporations they deal with. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. I In less than a decade, telehealth has expanded from a sideshow of health care to an industry worth tens of billions of dollars. Companies like Hims & Hers and Teladoc have become household names, their ads interrupting streaming TV and flooding social media feeds with the promise of quick, convenient care. Despite their popularity, few patients understand who’s actually taking care of them when they click through a telehealth site. Advertisement National telehealth brands present a unified front to patients across the country. But in more than 30 states, it’s illegal for corporations to practice medicine. So behind the scenes, telehealth companies work with distinct, independent medical groups. Owned by physicians — who often hold 50 state licenses at once — those practices are meant to act as a firewall, making sure that clinical decisions are driven by patients’ needs, not the profits of the corporations they deal with. articles.title: Global coalition to fast-track three vaccines targeting Ebola outbreak with $62 million in funding
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus In the Lab Global coalition to fast-track three vaccines targeting Ebola outbreak with $62 million in funding Trials for drug to combat Bundibugyo in Democratic Republic of Congo probably months away Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article A doctor administers an Ebola vaccine in 2019. The ebolavirus now spreading in Democratic Republic of Congo is infrequently seen, so work on developing a vaccine is behind. PAMELA TULIZO / AFP via Getty Images By Helen Branswell June 1, 2026 Infectious Diseases Correspondent Helen Branswell Helen Branswell covers issues broadly related to infectious diseases, including outbreaks, preparedness, research, and vaccine development. Follow her on Mastodon and Bluesky . You can reach Helen on Signal at hbranswell.01. Get email alerts for Helen Branswell Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts With no licensed vaccines available to protect against the Ebola virus currently spreading in Democratic Republic of Congo, efforts are underway to fast-track development of at least three vaccines. But even with infusions of cash to help fund the work, it is likely to be months before clinical trials of vaccines that specifically target the Bundibugyo ebolavirus can begin. The Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) announced Monday that it is providing three entities with roughly $62 million in funding to help manufacture and test Bundibugyo vaccines. The only licensed Ebola vaccine, Merck’s Ervebo, targets the Zaire ebolavirus. Advertisement Bundibugyo virus has rarely caused outbreaks in the past; only two previous epidemics, in 2007 and 2012, have been recorded. Because it has been so infrequently seen, work to develop vaccines and therapeutics to use against this particular form of Ebola has trailed development of tools to combat other filoviruses — the family to which Ebola belongs — such as Sudan ebolavirus or Marburg, a separate virus that triggers disease similar to that caused by Ebola viruses. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. STAT Plus In the Lab Global coalition to fast-track three vaccines targeting Ebola outbreak with $62 million in funding Trials for drug to combat Bundibugyo in Democratic Republic of Congo probably months away Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article A doctor administers an Ebola vaccine in 2019. The ebolavirus now spreading in Democratic Republic of Congo is infrequently seen, so work on developing a vaccine is behind. PAMELA TULIZO / AFP via Getty Images By Helen Branswell June 1, 2026 Infectious Diseases Correspondent Helen Branswell Helen Branswell covers issues broadly related to infectious diseases, including outbreaks, preparedness, research, and vaccine development. Follow her on Mastodon and Bluesky . You can reach Helen on Signal at hbranswell.01. Get email alerts for Helen Branswell Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts With no licensed vaccines available to protect against the Ebola virus currently spreading in Democratic Republic of Congo, efforts are underway to fast-track development of at least three vaccines. But even with infusions of cash to help fund the work, it is likely to be months before clinical trials of vaccines that specifically target the Bundibugyo ebolavirus can begin. The Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) announced Monday that it is providing three entities with roughly $62 million in funding to help manufacture and test Bundibugyo vaccines. The only licensed Ebola vaccine, Merck’s Ervebo, targets the Zaire ebolavirus. Advertisement Bundibugyo virus has rarely caused outbreaks in the past; only two previous epidemics, in 2007 and 2012, have been recorded. Because it has been so infrequently seen, work to develop vaccines and therapeutics to use against this particular form of Ebola has trailed development of tools to combat other filoviruses — the family to which Ebola belongs — such as Sudan ebolavirus or Marburg, a separate virus that triggers disease similar to that caused by Ebola viruses. articles.title: As FDA misses deadline on electric shock ban, disability advocates speak out
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Health As FDA misses deadline on electric shock ban, disability advocates speak out The agency has sought to ban its use in the past but had to reverse course after a court intervened Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Sarah Silbiger/Getty Images By O. Rose Broderick June 1, 2026 Disability in Health Care Reporting Fellow O. Rose Broderick rose.broderick@statnews.com Rose covers disability and mental health, and she helps write STAT’s daily Morning Rounds newsletter . Confidential tips can be sent to Rose on Signal at rosebroderick.11. Get email alerts for O. Rose Broderick Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Two years ago, the Food and Drug Administration gave itself a deadline. The agency would eventually decide whether to ban electrical shock devices that have been used for decades to manage self-injurious behavior in people with intellectual disabilities and autism. The deadline, pegged to the end of May, has now passed without a verdict , leaving disability rights activists and former recipients of these shocks worried that they will continue. The practice — dubbed a form of “ torture ” by United Nations officials and “ punishing ” by the American Academy of Pediatrics — has mostly fallen out of favor in the United States in recent decades but is still used at one institution: the Judge Rotenberg Center in Massachusetts. Advertisement Disability advocates have been trying to shut down JRC for decades, especially after a video of a resident being shocked for seven hours was shown in court in 2012. The institution offers schooling in addition to therapy, but it is mostly seen as a last resort and sought after for its use of behavioral treatments for kids and adults who have not responded to other types of therapy. disability , FDA , Policy Submit a correction request Reprints O. Rose Broderick Disability in Health Care Reporting Fellow Rose covers disability and mental health, and she helps write STAT’s daily Morning Rounds newsletter . Confidential tips can be sent to Rose on Signal at rosebroderick.11. Two years ago, the Food and Drug Administration gave itself a deadline. The agency would eventually decide whether to ban electrical shock devices that have been used for decades to manage self-injurious behavior in people with intellectual disabilities and autism. The deadline, pegged to the end of May, has now passed without a verdict , leaving disability rights activists and former recipients of these shocks worried that they will continue. The practice — dubbed a form of “ torture ” by United Nations officials and “ punishing ” by the American Academy of Pediatrics — has mostly fallen out of favor in the United States in recent decades but is still used at one institution: the Judge Rotenberg Center in Massachusetts. Advertisement Disability advocates have been trying to shut down JRC for decades, especially after a video of a resident being shocked for seven hours was shown in court in 2012. The institution offers schooling in addition to therapy, but it is mostly seen as a last resort and sought after for its use of behavioral treatments for kids and adults who have not responded to other types of therapy.

articles.title: Medicare's mum on GLP-1s, and Massachusetts sues United
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Newsletter Health Care Inc. Newsletter Medicare’s mum on GLP-1s, and Massachusetts sues United You’re reading STAT’s Health Care Inc. newsletter Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Scott Olson/Getty Images By Bob Herman June 1, 2026 Business of Health Care Reporter Bob Herman bob.herman@statnews.com Bob covers the money in health care, focusing on health insurance and hospitals. His stories delve into Medicare Advantage, opaque prescription drug benefits, and how much executives actually make. He is also the author of the Health Care Inc. newsletter . You can reach Bob on Signal at bobjherman.09. Get email alerts for Bob Herman Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts This is the online version of STAT’s weekly email newsletter Health Care Inc. Sign up here . Hello, and all hail Chonkers ! Get your Alice Cooper voice ready, because school’s out for summer . But HCI is open year-round, so tell me all about your summer plans and any juicy intel you have on the health care industry: bob.herman@statnews.com . Advertisement Radio silence on obesity coverage cost Medicare is advertising that older adults can get Wegovy and Zepbound, specifically for weight loss, starting this July for $50 a month. But the government still is not sharing how much this will cost taxpayers , who will be footing most of the bill. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: What should hospitals do when patients demand unvaccinated donor blood?
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion Podcast What should hospitals do when patients demand unvaccinated donor blood? A rare but real phenomenon creates new challenges for doctors, blood banks, and medical systems Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article By Torie Bosch June 1, 2026 Editor, First Opinion Torie Bosch torie.bosch@statnews.com Torie Bosch is the First Opinion editor at STAT. Get email alerts for Torie Bosch Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Below is a lightly edited, AI-generated transcript of the “First Opinion Podcast” interview with Deva Sharma. Be sure to sign up for the weekly “First Opinion Podcast” on Apple Podcasts , Spotify , or wherever you get your podcasts. Get alerts about each new episode by signing up for the “First Opinion Podcast” newsletter . And don’t forget to sign up for the First Opinion newsletter , delivered every Sunday. For many people, there’s something a little unsettling about the idea of a blood transfusion. It’s someone else’s cells becoming part of their body. And in the aftermath of the Covid pandemic, some patients, including parents of patients, are objecting to the idea of a donation from someone who has been vaccinated. But how can and should hospitals respond to these requests? Advertisement Welcome to the “First Opinion Podcast.” I’m Torie Bosch, editor of First Opinion. articles.title: Q&A: How Direct Access and Transparency Close the Patient Gap
ThermoSafe Expands US-Based VIP Manufacturing
First Oral Post-Exposure Covid-19 Prevention Drug Approved By FDA
Minimum Viable Launch, Maximum Patient Pull-Through
Tiny HHS office tasked with protecting research participants' safety is running on fumes
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article By Megan Molteni June 5, 2026 Megan Molteni megan.molteni@statnews.com Megan covers the intersection of science and society. How is biomedical research funded? Who benefits? And how do new technologies reflect and shape our values? You can reach Megan on Signal at mmolteni.13. Get email alerts for Megan Molteni Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts In 2021, the federal office charged with ensuring that the vast research enterprise bankrolled by the Department of Health and Human Services keeps study participants safe, received a report of a death by suicide involving a person enrolled in a study testing a treatment for depression and reduced mobility. Once the Office of Human Research Protections started looking into the death at the New York State Psychiatric Institute, it found problems that spread far beyond that one study, including failures in how the institute’s ethics board reviews proposed research. “We saw, holy smokes, this whole institution has issues,” said Lisa Buchanan, then director of the OHRP’s compliance division. The investigation became all-consuming. “It was probably 60-70% of our time,” she said. Advertisement The Food and Drug Administration got involved, as did the National Institutes of Health, which had provided grant funding to support the study. OHRP put a hold on all HHS-funded clinical research being conducted at NYSPI, and shortly after, the NIH suspended all grants for human subject research at the institute. Such severe actions are rare in the world of biomedical research. Clinical trials , ethics , HHS , NIH , research Submit a correction request Reprints Megan Molteni Science Writer Megan covers the intersection of science and society. How is biomedical research funded? Who benefits? And how do new technologies reflect and shape our values? You can reach Megan on Signal at mmolteni.13. Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article By Megan Molteni June 5, 2026 Megan Molteni megan.molteni@statnews.com Megan covers the intersection of science and society. How is biomedical research funded? Who benefits? And how do new technologies reflect and shape our values? You can reach Megan on Signal at mmolteni.13. Get email alerts for Megan Molteni Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts In 2021, the federal office charged with ensuring that the vast research enterprise bankrolled by the Department of Health and Human Services keeps study participants safe, received a report of a death by suicide involving a person enrolled in a study testing a treatment for depression and reduced mobility. Once the Office of Human Research Protections started looking into the death at the New York State Psychiatric Institute, it found problems that spread far beyond that one study, including failures in how the institute’s ethics board reviews proposed research. “We saw, holy smokes, this whole institution has issues,” said Lisa Buchanan, then director of the OHRP’s compliance division. The investigation became all-consuming. “It was probably 60-70% of our time,” she said. Advertisement The Food and Drug Administration got involved, as did the National Institutes of Health, which had provided grant funding to support the study. OHRP put a hold on all HHS-funded clinical research being conducted at NYSPI, and shortly after, the NIH suspended all grants for human subject research at the institute. Such severe actions are rare in the world of biomedical research. articles.title: Drug companies, patient groups urge FDA to pause commissioner's voucher program
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Politics Drug companies, patient groups urge FDA to pause commissioner’s voucher program At town hall, experts recommend agency formalize and clarify the speedy drug review pathway Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Jose Luis Magana/AP By Lizzy Lawrence June 4, 2026 FDA Reporter Lizzy Lawrence lizzy.lawrence@statnews.com Lizzy leads STAT’s coverage of the FDA. Her stories explore the relationship between politics and science at the FDA, industry influence, and the agency’s ability to protect and promote public health. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at lizzylaw.53. Get email alerts for Lizzy Lawrence Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts WASHINGTON — Leaders at the Food and Drug Administration on Thursday listened to criticisms and recommendations for how to move forward with a speedy drug review program put in place by former FDA commissioner Marty Makary. The listening session, held on the FDA’s White Oak Campus, featured 17 speakers representing patient groups, drug companies, and academic organizations. Some had positive feedback, particularly those whose drugs have already been approved through the program. But most asked the agency to pause the program, and then bring it back through normal regulatory procedures that require public feedback. Advertisement Makary launched the Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher program about a year ago, offering one- to two-month FDA reviews to companies that could prove their drugs “align with national priorities.” The priorities, which included addressing health crises and delivering innovative cures, were vague. Critics worried the process was vulnerable to political interference. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: After hospitals, patients get a turn to bring AI into the doctor's office
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Health tech After hospitals, patients get a turn to bring AI into the doctor’s office Developers pitch their patient apps as diligent note-takers to keep track of encounters across the health system Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Katie Palmer June 4, 2026 Health Tech Correspondent Katie Palmer katie.palmer@statnews.com Katie covers the impact of health technology on patients, clinicians, and businesses. Her stories explore the price tag of clinical AI, digital health at the FDA, and the boom in direct-to-consumer telehealth. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at palmer.01. Get email alerts for Katie Palmer Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Patients are getting used to being recorded in the doctor’s office. More than a quarter of U.S. practices now use AI-based listening tools called ambient scribes, which capture visits in real time and draft clinical notes for clinicians to enter into patients’ medical records. But what happens when it’s the patient doing the recording? Advertisement Nearly every patient now has a recording device in their pocket, and commercial large language models have made it easier than ever for them to transcribe and interpret audio files. A growing number of consumer apps — with names like VisitRecall, Advoca Health, and AlignCare — are using AI to do just that, delivering summaries and action items for patients after the fact. One called Kin Health, with backing from the co-founder of GoodRx, announced a $9 million seed round in May. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: Lawmakers promised cancer patients would be protected from Medicaid cuts. Now CMS says otherwise
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion Lawmakers promised cancer patients would be protected from Medicaid cuts. Now CMS says otherwise New work requirements are catastrophic Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Chris Hondros/Getty Images By Gwen Nichols June 4, 2026 Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United. In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions. One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition. Advertisement Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage. advocacy , Cancer , CMS , HHS , Medicaid Submit a correction request Reprints Gwen Nichols Newsletter The smartest thinkers in life sciences on what's happening — and what's to come Advertisement Most Popular Top ultra-processed food researchers call for sweeping policy change: ‘The system is rigged’ Revolution Medicines starts shipping experimental pancreatic cancer drug Trump administration to strip job protections of top NIH officials, grants staff Longevity startup NewLimit raises $435 million ahead of first clinical trial The virtual end of the doctor’s office waiting room 2025 Edition: Ranking Biotech’s Top Venture Capital Firms Advertisement STAT@ASCO: Science vs. Cancer Chicago, IL May 29 ASCO 2026: The STAT Recap Virtual Jun 3 2026 Breakthrough Summit West San Francisco, CA May 19 Most Read in STAT+ Revolution Medicines starts shipping experimental pancreatic cancer drug For prostate cancer patients set on surgery, new hormone regimen may improve outcomes, study finds Trump administration seeks to overhaul federal grantmaking process, alarming researchers Blood pressure tech floods the market after FDA relaxes wearables oversight Practice-changing results reported for Revolution Medicines pancreatic cancer drug Recommended First Opinion First Opinion June 4, 2026 Grail’s multi-cancer early detection trial was negative. In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions. One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. Advertisement Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. By Gwen Nichols
Gwen Nichols
June 4, 2026
Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United.
In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions. One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition. Advertisement Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage.
In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions.
75 million Americans
One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition.
Advertisement
Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage.

articles.title: What the pope's encyclical on AI means for Catholic hospitals, and all of health care
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Newsletter AI Prognosis What the pope’s encyclical on AI means for Catholic hospitals, and all of health care You’re reading the web edition of STAT AI Prognosis Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article STAT/ Alberto PIZZOLI / AFP via Getty Images By Brittany Trang June 3, 2026 Health Tech Reporter Brittany Trang brittany.trang@statnews.com Brittany Trang, Ph.D., covers AI in health and medicine: Does it actually work? Who benefits, or might be harmed? She writes the weekly AI Prognosis newsletter . Follow her on Threads , Mastodon , and Bluesky . You can reach Brittany on Signal at btrang.01. Get email alerts for Brittany Trang Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Y ou’re reading the web edition of STAT’s AI Prognosis newsletter, our subscriber-exclusive guide to artificial intelligence in health care and medicine. Sign up to get it delivered in your inbox every Wednesday. The sequel to last week’s graduation speeches that garnered boos for AI: Comedian Ronny Chieng addressed Harvard’s graduating class and told them their mission was to “destroy AI,” which prompted a roar of approval. Advertisement He wasn’t referring to uses of AI in medicine and physics, he specified, but rather offloading writing and creating to AI. The journey of making and learning and figuring out is “the point of all of this,” he said. “Why would I want AI to take that away from me?” To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: The medical-billing AI arms race between providers and insurance
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion The medical-billing AI arms race between providers and insurance Both sides are training algorithms to fight over the same patient record Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Darshak Sanghavi June 3, 2026 Sanghavi, a pediatric cardiologist and former senior federal health official, is chief medical officer of Machinify. In November 2020, Bisi Bennett went into labor at seven months pregnant. Halfway to AdventHealth hospital, with her husband, Chris, behind the wheel, she gave birth to their son Dorian in their Mitsubishi Outlander. He didn’t breathe. At the hospital, before they wheeled Bisi away from her newborn, she heard four magical words: “We’ve got a pulse.” Dorian spent 56 days in the neonatal intensive care unit getting respiratory support, surgery, cardiology, and nutritional care, and thrived. The doctors and nurses had, by any measure, performed a small miracle. Advertisement It’s hard to put a price on a miracle. But technology has increasingly taken on the challenge. articles.title: Trump's Medicaid work requirements have an unwelcome surprise for some states and patients
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Politics Trump’s Medicaid work requirements have an unwelcome surprise for some states and patients New rules will make it far harder to get Medicaid and stay covered Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Damian Dovarganes/AP By Tara Bannow , Chelsea Cirruzzo ,  and Daniel Payne June 3, 2026 Tara Bannow tara.bannow@statnews.com Tara covers the business of health care. Her stories focus on hospitals, doctors, and how their business practices affect patients — especially when private equity gets involved. She also writes about health insurance and ideas for improving our broken health system. You can reach Tara on Signal at tarabannow.70. Get email alerts for Tara Bannow Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Chelsea Cirruzzo chelsea.cirruzzo@statnews.com Chelsea covers the Department of Health and Human Services with a particular focus on power and influence at the top. Confidential tips can be sent via Signal at chelseacirruzzo.42. Get email alerts for Chelsea Cirruzzo Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Daniel Payne daniel.payne@statnews.com Daniel covers the intersection of the health industry and the federal government. He reports on corporate influence in government, the health consequences of federal policies, and the politics of health care. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at danielp.100. Get email alerts for Daniel Payne Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Medicaid leaders and advocates say they’re shocked by the Trump administration’s harsh directives for implementing Medicaid work requirements, which they say mark a pivot from how federal officials had characterized their plans just weeks ago. Much of the conversation around the nearly 400-page rule that the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services released Monday afternoon centers on one of the ways that people can be exempted from work requirements: medical frailty. Getting that exemption will be more difficult than most people had expected, meaning that more sick and disabled people are likely to lose their Medicaid coverage. Advertisement “This is where we’ll see large and harmful coverage losses,” said Benjamin Sommers, an economics professor at the Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health. “This is a population that has high medical needs and is at major risk for harm if they lose coverage. That is the headline implication of the new rule.” To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. Subscribe Log In CMS , Congress , disability , Medicaid , patients , Policy , public health , STAT+ Submit a correction request Reprints Tara Bannow Hospitals and Insurance Reporter Tara covers the business of health care. Her stories focus on hospitals, doctors, and how their business practices affect patients — especially when private equity gets involved. She also writes about health insurance and ideas for improving our broken health system. You can reach Tara on Signal at tarabannow.70. Chelsea Cirruzzo Washington Correspondent Chelsea covers the Department of Health and Human Services with a particular focus on power and influence at the top. Confidential tips can be sent via Signal at chelseacirruzzo.42. Daniel Payne Washington Correspondent Daniel covers the intersection of the health industry and the federal government. He reports on corporate influence in government, the health consequences of federal policies, and the politics of health care. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at danielp.100. Getting that exemption will be more difficult than most people had expected, meaning that more sick and disabled people are likely to lose their Medicaid coverage. articles.title: Online care is caught in the crossfire as states crack down on corporate medicine
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Thumy Phan for STAT STAT Plus Health tech Online care is caught in the crossfire as states crack down on corporate medicine By Katie Palmer June 2, 2026 Palmer is investigating how telehealth is driving the consumerization of drugs. This is Part 5 of the series The VirtualRx Boom. Katie Palmer katie.palmer@statnews.com Katie covers the impact of health technology on patients, clinicians, and businesses. Her stories explore the price tag of clinical AI, digital health at the FDA, and the boom in direct-to-consumer telehealth. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at palmer.01. Get email alerts for Katie Palmer Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article I In less than a decade, telehealth has expanded from a sideshow of health care to an industry worth tens of billions of dollars. Companies like Hims & Hers and Teladoc have become household names, their ads interrupting streaming TV and flooding social media feeds with the promise of quick, convenient care. Despite their popularity, few patients understand who’s actually taking care of them when they click through a telehealth site. Advertisement National telehealth brands present a unified front to patients across the country. But in more than 30 states, it’s illegal for corporations to practice medicine. So behind the scenes, telehealth companies work with distinct, independent medical groups. Owned by physicians — who often hold 50 state licenses at once — those practices are meant to act as a firewall, making sure that clinical decisions are driven by patients’ needs, not the profits of the corporations they deal with. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: Global coalition to fast-track three vaccines targeting Ebola outbreak with $62 million in funding
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus In the Lab Global coalition to fast-track three vaccines targeting Ebola outbreak with $62 million in funding Trials for drug to combat Bundibugyo in Democratic Republic of Congo probably months away Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article A doctor administers an Ebola vaccine in 2019. The ebolavirus now spreading in Democratic Republic of Congo is infrequently seen, so work on developing a vaccine is behind. PAMELA TULIZO / AFP via Getty Images By Helen Branswell June 1, 2026 Infectious Diseases Correspondent Helen Branswell Helen Branswell covers issues broadly related to infectious diseases, including outbreaks, preparedness, research, and vaccine development. Follow her on Mastodon and Bluesky . You can reach Helen on Signal at hbranswell.01. Get email alerts for Helen Branswell Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts With no licensed vaccines available to protect against the Ebola virus currently spreading in Democratic Republic of Congo, efforts are underway to fast-track development of at least three vaccines. But even with infusions of cash to help fund the work, it is likely to be months before clinical trials of vaccines that specifically target the Bundibugyo ebolavirus can begin. The Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) announced Monday that it is providing three entities with roughly $62 million in funding to help manufacture and test Bundibugyo vaccines. The only licensed Ebola vaccine, Merck’s Ervebo, targets the Zaire ebolavirus. Advertisement Bundibugyo virus has rarely caused outbreaks in the past; only two previous epidemics, in 2007 and 2012, have been recorded. Because it has been so infrequently seen, work to develop vaccines and therapeutics to use against this particular form of Ebola has trailed development of tools to combat other filoviruses — the family to which Ebola belongs — such as Sudan ebolavirus or Marburg, a separate virus that triggers disease similar to that caused by Ebola viruses. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: As FDA misses deadline on electric shock ban, disability advocates speak out
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Health As FDA misses deadline on electric shock ban, disability advocates speak out The agency has sought to ban its use in the past but had to reverse course after a court intervened Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Sarah Silbiger/Getty Images By O. Rose Broderick June 1, 2026 Disability in Health Care Reporting Fellow O. Rose Broderick rose.broderick@statnews.com Rose covers disability and mental health, and she helps write STAT’s daily Morning Rounds newsletter . Confidential tips can be sent to Rose on Signal at rosebroderick.11. Get email alerts for O. Rose Broderick Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Two years ago, the Food and Drug Administration gave itself a deadline. The agency would eventually decide whether to ban electrical shock devices that have been used for decades to manage self-injurious behavior in people with intellectual disabilities and autism. The deadline, pegged to the end of May, has now passed without a verdict , leaving disability rights activists and former recipients of these shocks worried that they will continue. The practice — dubbed a form of “ torture ” by United Nations officials and “ punishing ” by the American Academy of Pediatrics — has mostly fallen out of favor in the United States in recent decades but is still used at one institution: the Judge Rotenberg Center in Massachusetts. Advertisement Disability advocates have been trying to shut down JRC for decades, especially after a video of a resident being shocked for seven hours was shown in court in 2012. The institution offers schooling in addition to therapy, but it is mostly seen as a last resort and sought after for its use of behavioral treatments for kids and adults who have not responded to other types of therapy. disability , FDA , Policy Submit a correction request Reprints O. Two years ago, the Food and Drug Administration gave itself a deadline. The agency would eventually decide whether to ban electrical shock devices that have been used for decades to manage self-injurious behavior in people with intellectual disabilities and autism. The deadline, pegged to the end of May, has now passed without a verdict , leaving disability rights activists and former recipients of these shocks worried that they will continue. The practice — dubbed a form of “ torture ” by United Nations officials and “ punishing ” by the American Academy of Pediatrics — has mostly fallen out of favor in the United States in recent decades but is still used at one institution: the Judge Rotenberg Center in Massachusetts. Advertisement Disability advocates have been trying to shut down JRC for decades, especially after a video of a resident being shocked for seven hours was shown in court in 2012. The institution offers schooling in addition to therapy, but it is mostly seen as a last resort and sought after for its use of behavioral treatments for kids and adults who have not responded to other types of therapy.

articles.title: Medicare's mum on GLP-1s, and Massachusetts sues United
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Newsletter Health Care Inc. Newsletter Medicare’s mum on GLP-1s, and Massachusetts sues United You’re reading STAT’s Health Care Inc. newsletter Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Scott Olson/Getty Images By Bob Herman June 1, 2026 Business of Health Care Reporter Bob Herman bob.herman@statnews.com Bob covers the money in health care, focusing on health insurance and hospitals. His stories delve into Medicare Advantage, opaque prescription drug benefits, and how much executives actually make. He is also the author of the Health Care Inc. newsletter . You can reach Bob on Signal at bobjherman.09. Get email alerts for Bob Herman Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts This is the online version of STAT’s weekly email newsletter Health Care Inc. Sign up here . Hello, and all hail Chonkers ! Get your Alice Cooper voice ready, because school’s out for summer . But HCI is open year-round, so tell me all about your summer plans and any juicy intel you have on the health care industry: bob.herman@statnews.com . Advertisement Radio silence on obesity coverage cost Medicare is advertising that older adults can get Wegovy and Zepbound, specifically for weight loss, starting this July for $50 a month. But the government still is not sharing how much this will cost taxpayers , who will be footing most of the bill. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: What should hospitals do when patients demand unvaccinated donor blood?
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion Podcast What should hospitals do when patients demand unvaccinated donor blood? A rare but real phenomenon creates new challenges for doctors, blood banks, and medical systems Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article By Torie Bosch June 1, 2026 Editor, First Opinion Torie Bosch torie.bosch@statnews.com Torie Bosch is the First Opinion editor at STAT. Get email alerts for Torie Bosch Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Below is a lightly edited, AI-generated transcript of the “First Opinion Podcast” interview with Deva Sharma. Be sure to sign up for the weekly “First Opinion Podcast” on Apple Podcasts , Spotify , or wherever you get your podcasts. Get alerts about each new episode by signing up for the “First Opinion Podcast” newsletter . And don’t forget to sign up for the First Opinion newsletter , delivered every Sunday. For many people, there’s something a little unsettling about the idea of a blood transfusion. It’s someone else’s cells becoming part of their body. And in the aftermath of the Covid pandemic, some patients, including parents of patients, are objecting to the idea of a donation from someone who has been vaccinated. But how can and should hospitals respond to these requests? Advertisement Welcome to the “First Opinion Podcast.” I’m Torie Bosch, editor of First Opinion. First Opinion is STAT’s home for big, bold ideas from health care providers, researchers, patients, and others who have something to say about medicine’s most important and interesting topics. This season, we’re focused on the intersection of medicine and culture. children’s health , ethics Submit a correction request Reprints Torie Bosch Editor, First Opinion Torie Bosch is the First Opinion editor at STAT. For many people, there’s something a little unsettling about the idea of a blood transfusion. It’s someone else’s cells becoming part of their body. And in the aftermath of the Covid pandemic, some patients, including parents of patients, are objecting to the idea of a donation from someone who has been vaccinated. But how can and should hospitals respond to these requests? Advertisement Welcome to the “First Opinion Podcast.” I’m Torie Bosch, editor of First Opinion. First Opinion is STAT’s home for big, bold ideas from health care providers, researchers, patients, and others who have something to say about medicine’s most important and interesting topics. This season, we’re focused on the intersection of medicine and culture.

For many people, there’s something a little unsettling about the idea of a blood transfusion. It’s someone else’s cells becoming part of their body. And in the aftermath of the Covid pandemic, some patients, including parents of patients, are objecting to the idea of a donation from someone who has been vaccinated. But how can and should hospitals respond to these requests?
